segments.s320-g89: نگه‌دار is a vocative noun here ('O guardian/preserver') addressing the addressee; it could also be read as an imperative in other contexts, but poetic address is common. بر من is a prepositional phrase ('over/on me' — بر + object pronoun من) specifying who is to be guarded. همین راه و سان is the thing being requested to be preserved: همین ('this same') modifies راه ('path/way') and سان ('manner/way') joined by the conjunction و, with سان an older/poetic word meaning 'manner' or 'as such'.
segments.s320-g90: روانم is 'my soul' (روان with the possessive suffix -م). ب + دان here functions as the classical/poetic compound بدان meaning 'to that' (ب = to/into plus دان as a demonstrative; the demonstrative/datative sense is an older usage). جای نیکان = 'the place of the righteous/good' (جای 'place' governing نیکان 'the good', with the ezāfe often omitted in poetry). رسان is an imperative/optative verb (from رساندن) meaning 'bring' or 'cause to reach', placed at the end in typical Persian SOV order and commanding the addressee to bring the speaker's soul to that place.
segments.s320-g25: This is a poetic, inverted clause: the prepositional phrase ز من ('from me') comes first, then the verb بگسلد (3rd singular present/subjunctive/optative), and finally the noun phrase فره ایزدی ('divine farr') as the thing acted on. بگسلد here carries the sense 'be torn / be broken off' (a result caused by the conditional clause that follows).
segments.s320-g26: گر introduces the protasis 'if' and is followed by آیم (1st person singular present/subjunctive of آمدن, 'I come / I turn / I fall into'). به governs the following nouns and here means 'into/toward' (indicating movement or turning into a state). کژی و راه بدی is a coordinate nominal phrase 'crookedness and an evil way/path' describing the illicit course.
segments.s320-g27: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (از + demonstrative + پس). بر آن تیرگی is a prepositional phrase 'over/above that darkness' and بگذرم is 1st singular present/subjunctive (optative) of گذشتن used to express the speaker's hope/wish or a future result: 'may I pass / I will pass'.
segments.s320-g28: This clause uses the locative-into construction به خاک اندر آید: به + خاک + اندر (in/into) + آید (3rd singular of آمدن) to express 'fall into / come into the dust'. سر و افسرم is a coordinated nominal phrase 'head and my afsar(=helmet)', with افسرm bearing the possessive suffix '‑م' ('my'). The whole means 'my head and my helmet will come to the earth' (i.e. fall).
segments.s320-g29: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (به + گیتی); بماند is 3rd singular present/subjunctive of ماندن used to indicate future/result 'will remain'. ز من 'from me' functions as a genitive-like phrase 'of me' (i.e. 'my'); نام بد 'bad name/reputation' is the thing that will remain. Word order is topicalized (location first).
segments.s320-g30: همان 'the same' or 'that same' modifies what follows; پیش یزدان is a prepositional/locative phrase 'before/in the presence of God' (پیش = 'before, in the presence of'); سرانجام بد places the predicate at the end: 'the outcome/end will be bad'. The verb is omitted and understood from context (typical of poetic style).
segments.s320-g31: تبه گرددم is a compound predicate: تبه (an adjective/label meaning 'ruined/evil') + گرددم (1st singular past/present form meaning 'I became/turned'). In classical usage گرددن can mean 'to become' — here 'I became ruined' or 'I am ruined'. چهر و رنگ رخان is a coordinated noun phrase 'face and complexion (رخان: relaxed/loosened)', describing the physical change; the order places the state after the verb.
segments.s320-g32: بریزد is 3rd singular present/subjunctive of ریختن ('to fall, to be scattered') used here for a future/result: 'will fall / will be scattered'. The phrase به خاک اندرون استخوان places the goal/ground first then the affected thing: 'into the earth (به خاک اندرون) (the) bones (استخوان)'; idiomatically, 'my bones will be scattered into the ground.'
segments.s320-g17: شوم is the optative/subjunctive first‑person form of شدن (here 'may I become'); it expresses a wish or dreaded possibility. همچو 'like' precedes its noun complement (ضحاک تازی و جم), so the phrase literally reads 'may I become like Zahhāk the Arab and Jam.'
segments.s320-g18: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who / who are such that'). با + names (با سلم و تور) means 'with Salm and Tūr'; اندر آیم is subjunctive/relative 'I/they come into' (آیم here = 'come'), and به زم functions adverbially 'into the world / onto the earth' (here rendered 'into the world'). Word order is classical: preposition + objects, then verb, then temporal/location adverb.
segments.s320-g19: به یک‌سو 'to one side' = 'on one hand'. چو here functions as 'if/when' or 'as' introducing the example ('as / if I have Kāvus'). کاووس دارم uses دارم 'I have/possess' with نیا as its complement: 'I have Kāvus as ancestor' (نیا = 'ancestor').
segments.s320-g20: دگر سو 'the other side' = 'on the other hand.' چو = 'as/like,' introducing a comparison to توران (Turan). پر + از + noun is the idiom 'full of (something)' (پر از کیمیا = 'full of alchemy/magic').
segments.s320-g21: چو ... و چون ... is a repeated simile: 'as Kāvus and as (the) magic (of) Afrāsiāb.' جادو before the name Afra­siāb qualifies him ('magic/Afreasiab the sorcerer'); word order places the descriptive noun before the proper name in a poetic, appositional construction.
segments.s320-g22: که begins a relative clause 'who/which.' جز = 'except'; روی 'face/appearance' and کژی 'crookedness/wrongness' form the object 'nothing but a crooked face.' ندیدی is a past negative form of دیدن ('did not see'); به خواب is an adverbial phrase 'in sleep' / 'in a dream.' Together: 'who in a dream saw nothing but a crooked visage.'
segments.s320-g23: به یزدان is an invocation 'before God / to God.' شوم is the optative/subjunctive 'may I become.' یک زمان 'one moment' qualifies the duration. ناسپاس is predicate/adjective 'ungrateful.' The sequence gives the wish: 'may I, before God, for a moment become ungrateful.'
segments.s320-g24: به روشن‌روان اندر آرم هراس: به + روشن‌روان marks the (moral/spiritual) target 'toward the bright‑souled'; اندر 'into' + verb آرم (subjunctive/present 'I place/may I place') and object هراس 'fear.' The clause means 'may I put/bring fear into the bright‑souled' (i.e., cause fear in the radiant spirit).
segments.s320-g33: هنر is the grammatical subject ('virtue' or 'excellence'). کم شود is a predicative phrase: کم (diminished/insufficient) + شود (present/past‑resultative of شدن, 'becomes') so together 'becomes diminished'. ناسپاسی به جای is a noun phrase meaning 'ingratitude in place (of it)' where به جای is the fixed prepositional expression 'in place of / instead of'. The clause therefore reads literally 'virtue becomes diminished; ingratitude (comes) in its place' with the copular sense of شود implying 'takes place/appears'.
segments.s320-g34: روان is the noun 'spirit/soul'. تیره گردد is another predicative unit: تیره (dark/bleak) + گردد (from گردیدن used like شدن, 'become/turn'), so 'the soul becomes dark.' به دیگر سرای is a destination/locative phrase 'to another house/abode/realm' (به = to/into, دیگر = other/another, سرای = 'abode' here); the verb گرد(یدن) takes that phrase as indicating where or what the change leads to.
segments.s320-g35: گرفته is the perfect participle 'taken/seized' (used here as a finite predication: 'has taken'). کسی is the indefinite subject 'someone'. تاج و تخت مرا is the compound object 'my crown and throne' with مرا (= me + object marker را) marking possession/definite object. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (participle + subject + object), but the sense is 'someone has taken my crown and throne.'
segments.s320-g36: به پای اندر آورده is a verbal phrase meaning 'has brought (something) to the (one's) feet', where به پای اندر (به + پای + اندر) expresses the notion 'at/to the feet (of someone)' and آورده is the perfect participle 'brought/has brought'. بخت مرا is the object 'my fortune/lot' (بخت = fate/fortune, مرا = my / me + object marker). The whole means 'has brought my fortune to (their) feet' i.e. has humbled/subjugated my fortune.
segments.s320-g37: ز من نام ماند is literal word order 'from me name remained' (ز = 'from/of', من = 'me/I', نام = 'name/reputation', ماند = 'remained/was left'), so 'what remains of me is a name/reputation.' بدی یادگار follows as a nominal phrase 'an evil/bad memorial' (بدی qualifies یادگار). The phrase expresses that only an evil memory/reputation is left behind.
segments.s320-g38: گل رنجهای کهن is a noun phrase 'the flower of ancient sufferings' (گل = 'flower', رنجهای = plural 'sufferings/hardships' with the plural suffix های, کهن = 'ancient/old'). گشته خار is the verb phrase 'has become a thorn' (گشته, perfect participle of شدن/گشتن, 'has become/turned into'; خار = 'thorn'). Word order places the subject phrase before the verb, then the predicate noun.
segments.s320-g39: من اکنون چو کین پدر خواستم is a temporal/subordinate clause: من اکنون = 'I now'; چو = 'when/as' introducing the clause; کین پدر = 'the revenge/vengeance of my father' (کین = vengeance, پدر = father); خواستم is past tense 'I wanted/sought.' Together: 'Now, when I sought (to obtain) my father's vengeance' (i.e. 'when I attempted to avenge my father').
segments.s320-g40: جهانی به خوبی بیاراستم is the main clause: جهانی = 'the world'/'the whole world'; به خوبی is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with goodness' (به used adverbially here), and بیاراستم (بی-آراستن in the 1st‑person past/subjunctive/optative form) carries the meaning 'I would have arranged/adorned/righted.' In classical/poetic Persian this form often expresses an ideal or counterfactual: 'I would have set the world right with goodness.'
segments.s320-g65: ببست is a past tense verb 'he shut/closed' placed before its direct object (typical in Classical Persian narrative). آن در is a noun phrase 'that door/gate' (در here is the noun 'door', not the preposition), followed by بارگاه کیان 'the court of the Kayanian kings' as a genitive modifier of 'در'. The sequence verb → object → modifier is normal in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s320-g66: خروشان is an adverbial/participial form meaning 'roaring' or 'with a roar' and modifies the verb بیامد 'came' (past). گشاده‌میان is an adjectival epithet 'open‑hearted / open‑bosomed' that characterises the arriver; word order places the manner (خروشان) before the verb and the descriptive adjective after the verb, a poetic arrangement.
segments.s320-g67: ز بهر is a poetic double prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' or 'because of'; it introduces the purpose. سر و تن 'head and body' is the coordinated direct object, and بشست is the past tense verb 'he washed' agreeing with the implied subject (third person).
segments.s320-g68: به here means 'by/with' and introduces the instrument 'شمع خرد' (literally 'candle of wisdom', where خرد = wisdom modifies شمع). راه یزدان 'the way/path of God' is the object pursued, and بجست is the past verb 'he sought' (sought/strived after). The phrase order places the instrumental phrase first, then the object, then the verb in a compact poetic line.
segments.s320-g69: بپوشید is the past tense 'he put on / he clothed himself'. پس is temporal 'then/afterwards'. جامه نو سپید is a noun with two postpositive adjectives (نو 'new', سپید 'white'), the usual adjective order in Persian poetry following the noun.
segments.s320-g70: نیایش کنان is an adverbial participle 'praying' (کنان = 'doing'), giving the manner of رفت 'he went' (past). دل پر امید is a noun phrase 'a heart full of hope' where پر is the adjective 'full (of)' modifying دل and followed by امید 'hope'.
segments.s320-g71: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. خرامان is an adverbial adjective 'stately / with a proud gait' modifying the verb. به جای نماز is a prepositional phrase 'to the place of prayer' (به = to; جای = place; نماز = prayer), giving the destination.
segments.s320-g72: همی is an archaic/prosodic particle that, with a verb form, gives iterative or continuous aspect ('kept' / 'was continually'). گفت here functions as the reported verb 'said/spoke' (note: the provided sense list for this token does not include the verbal sense, see senses mapping). با داور پاک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/with the pure Judge' (داور = judge, here a divine judge; پاک = pure), and راز is the direct object 'secret'. Word order is somewhat flexible in poetry, but the phrase expresses continuous speech directed to the divine judge.
segments.s320-g57: رسیدیم و دیدیم are two past-tense, first-person-plural verbs ('we arrived' / 'we saw') joined by the coordinating و. راز جهان is a genitive noun phrase 'the secret(s) of the world' (English 'the secrets of the world'); in poetry the ezāfe linking راز to جهان is omitted but the possessive relation is understood. The sequence places the two verbs before their object for emphasis: 'we arrived and (there) saw the world's secret.'
segments.s320-g58: This is a string of adjectives/qualifiers that belong to راز جهان from the previous segment: بد و نیک 'bad and good' and (هم) آشکار و نهان 'both manifest and hidden'. هم here functions distributively — 'both' — tying آشکار and نهان together; there is no copula because the adjectives qualify 'راز'.
segments.s320-g59: This line contrasts two social types (کشاورز 'peasant/farmer' and تاجور 'merchant/trader') with an archaic/distributive sense: the phrase should be read as 'whether we saw a farmer or a merchant.' The particle گر (here joined in the line) can mark an 'if/whether' or alternative, so the two nouns are being set in opposition or equivalence as objects of 'we saw' (the verb sense is carried over from the previous clause).
segments.s320-g60: سرانجام 'ultimately' is an adverbial; بر مرگ 'upon/to death' uses the preposition بر + noun مرگ. باشد گذر is a poetic, impersonal construction where باشد (subjunctive/optative of 'to be') and the noun گذر 'passage' express the idea 'there is a passing (to death)' or 'ultimately the passage is to death.' The clause states the universal outcome: all end in death.
segments.s320-g61: به سالار نوبت places the indirect object first ('to/for the commander of the watch/turn'); بفرمود is an honorific past tense meaning 'he ordered/commanded' (polite register), and شاه 'the king' appears after the verb (inverted word order common in poetry). The whole segment means 'The king commanded the chief of the watch.'
segments.s320-g62: The complement clause introduced by که gives the content of the king's order: که هر کس که آید بدین بارگاه 'that whoever comes to this palace.' هر کس که 'whoever' (every person who) is followed by آید (present/subjunctive 'comes'); بدین is the prepositional fusion of ب + این 'to this', and بارگاه is 'royal court/palace.' Note the double که: the first introduces the clause, the second is the relative/indefinite 'whoever.'
segments.s320-g63: ورا is the object pronoun 'to him / him' (lit. 'to that/that one'); بازگردان is an imperative 'return / send back' (second-person imperative of بازگرداندن). به نیکو سخن is an adjunct 'with kind/good speech' (نیکو modifies سخن); together the phrase is a polite instruction: 'Send him back with kind words.'
segments.s320-g64: همه مردمی 'all the people' is the object of the verb implied next; جوی here functions imperatively 'seek!' (poetic/archaic imperative related to جستن / جوییدن), so 'seek all the people.' و تندی مکن is a negative imperative: تندی 'harshness / fierceness' with مکن 'do not do' — 'and be not harsh' or 'do not be hasty/harsh.' The clause gives two commands: seek the people and do not be severe.
segments.s320-g73: همی گفت is a habitual/progressive marker + past verb, here translated 'kept saying' (همی conveys continuing action). کای is the vocative 'O', followed by the comparative phrase برتر از جان پاک 'loftier than the pure soul' (برتر 'higher/superior', از introduces the standard of comparison, جان پاک 'pure soul').
segments.s320-g74: برآرنده is an active participle/epithet 'bringer/raiser (one who brings forth)'. آتش 'fire' is the direct object of that epithet; از تیره خاک is a prepositional phrase 'from the dark earth' (تیره 'dark/black', خاک 'earth/soil'). The whole functions as a descriptive title addressed to the addressee.
segments.s320-g75: مرا بین is the imperative 'look at me' (مرا = 'me' as object; بین = imperative 'see/look'). The coordination و links to the next request. چندی خرد ده مرا is a request 'give me some little wisdom'—چندی 'some/a little', خرد 'wisdom', ده imperative 'give', and the final مرا repeats the object for emphasis.
segments.s320-g76: هم here means 'also' or 'in addition'. اندیشه نیک و بد is a noun phrase 'thought/awareness of good and bad' (نیک 'good', بد 'bad/evil'). ده مرا (imperative 'give me') is again an imperative request, with مرا marking the recipient.
segments.s320-g77: ترا is the object/goal 'to/for you'. تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that / in order that'. بباشم is a subjunctive/optative form 'that I may be' (archaic/poetic variant with the b- prefix), and نیایش کنم is a subordinate verb phrase 'I may perform/pray' with کنَم as the 1st-person subjunctive of کردن used to make the verbal noun 'نیایش' into 'to pray/perform supplication'.
segments.s320-g78: بدین is a prepositional/adverbial compound 'by/with this' (ب- 'by/with' + دین here functioning deictically as 'this' in the phrase). نیکویها is a plural/collective 'virtues/good things', and فزایش کنم is 'I will increase' or 'I will cause an increase' (فزایش 'increase', کنَم = 1st-person verb 'I do/make' in subjunctive/volitional sense). Together: 'By these virtues I will increase (my praise/devotion).'
segments.s320-g79: بیامرز is an imperative 'forgive' (a prayer/request form). رفته modifies گناه as a past/finished participle 'the sin that has gone/been committed', so رفته گناه = 'past sin' or 'sin already done'. مرا (mora) marks the possessor/affected party 'my.../me', with را the object-marking clitic.
segments.s320-g80: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. کژی means 'crookedness/wrongness' and is the source from which the speaker asks rescue. بکش is an imperative 'pull/draw away' (here 'deliver/bring away' rather than literal 'kill'), and دستگاه مرا 'my court/household/establishment' is the thing to be rescued; مرا/را again mark possession/object.
segments.s320-g49: کنون (an adverb) opens the clause: 'now / at this moment.' آن به آید که is a poetic periphrasis meaning 'it will now come to pass that...' (به آید = will happen/come), and که introduces the content clause. من راه‌جوی is the subject phrase ('I, a seeker') whose verb is postponed to the next segment (شوم).
segments.s320-g50: شوم is the first‑person subjunctive/jussive of شدن ('may I become' / 'I should become') completing the predicate started before; پیش یزدان is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the presence of God.' پر از آب روی is an adjectival phrase modifying the speaker's face: پر (full) + از آب (of water) + روی (face) = 'with a face full of water' (i.e. tearful).
segments.s320-g51: مگر here introduces a rhetorical question ('might there not / is it not the case that...'); هم adds 'also.' بدین خوبی is a locative phrase 'in/with this goodness' (ب- as prefix + this goodness), اندر نهان places that goodness 'within, secretly' (اندر = in, نهان = hidden).
segments.s320-g52: پرستنده is a noun 'worshipper / devotee' followed by the genitive/attributive phrase کردگار جهان = 'the Creator (کردگار) of the world (جهان),' so the whole phrase means 'a worshipper of the Creator of the world.' The words are juxtaposed in classical Persian to express possession/association.
segments.s320-g53: روانم 'my soul' (روان + enclitic ـم). بدان is a locative 'to that' (ب- prefix + دان/that), جای نیکان = 'the place of the good/righteous.' برد here is the verb 'carry / take' (an archaic/poetic form of بردن) giving the wish/optative meaning 'may (it) carry.' Word order places the destination phrase after the possessor noun.
segments.s320-g54: که introduces the reason/explanatory clause ('for / since / because'). این تاج و تخت مهی is a noun phrase 'this fair / moon‑like crown and throne' (مهی = moon‑like / beautiful) and بگذرد is the poetic present/subjunctive of گذشتن 'to pass' meaning 'will pass away / come to an end.'
segments.s320-g55: نیابد is the negative form of یابیدن here meaning 'does not obtain / cannot attain'; with the indefinite کسی the sense is 'no one' (negation + کسی = 'no one'). ز این فزون is 'from this excess/abundance,' and کام و نام lists the things not attained: 'desire (کام) and renown/reputation (نام).' In classical Persian the negative verb often precedes the subject.
segments.s320-g56: A continued list of nouns joined by و: بزرگی (greatness), خوبی (goodness), آرام (peace/calm), و جام (and the cup). The sequence enumerates further goods that no one attains from that excessive abundance; جام (cup) is metaphorical (drink/feast/celebration).
segments.s320-g41: بکشتم is an archaic past-tense verb form meaning “I killed” (the -م is the 1st‑person ending). The clause that follows is the direct object: کسی را (کسی + را = “someone” with the definite/object marker) and the relative/oblique clause introduced by که modifies that کسی: بایست (must/ought) + کشت (the past stem used like an infinitive here = ‘be killed’). Together the clause says “I killed someone who ought to be killed.”
segments.s320-g42: که continues the relative clause “who…” and introduces a string of adjectives. بد = “bad/evil” and کژ = “crooked/wrong, perverse”; they jointly describe that someone. و با راه یزدان درشت links another predicative phrase: با + راه یزدان = “with/regarding the way of God,” and درشت is an adjectival predicate “coarse/rude.” The whole line characterizes the person as evil, perverse, and rude toward God’s way.
segments.s320-g43: به آباد و ویران sets a locative frame “in the inhabited (places) and in the ruined (places).” درختی (an indefinite “a tree” or “any tree”) is the noun being quantified, and نماند is the negative past of ماندن “to remain/ be left” — literally “there remained not a tree.” This is a classical Persian rhetorical negative construction (no X remained that ...).
segments.s320-g44: که introduces a relative clause modifying درختی. منشور تخت = “the inscribed tablet/inscription of the throne,” while م + را together correspond to the classical clitic/morpheme rendering of “m-e/‑me” (here marking the possession/object: “my”/“of my”). برنخواند is a negative perfect/past form “did not read/recite.” The subordinate clause therefore means “that did not read (i.e. recite) the inscription of my throne,” and combined with the previous line it implies every tree did read it (double negative rhetorical effect).
segments.s320-g45: بزرگان گیتی = “the great ones of the world” (subject). م + را together encode the first‑person target “me” (here the object/comparison point). کهترند is a predicative adjective-verb meaning “are inferior/lesser.” Word order: subject — (me as the target/complement) — predicate “are inferior,” i.e. “the world’s great ones are inferior to me.”
segments.s320-g46: وگر چند is a concessive formula “and yet/and though” (وگر = ‘but/if’, چند here serves a concessive ‘though’ sense). The rest is a coordinated prepositional phrase with a nominal predicate: با گنج و با افسرند = “even if they have treasure and [wear] helmets.” با + noun = “with/possessing,” and افسرند is a plural predicate (they are/they have) based on افسر (helmet/armour). The clause concedes material wealth/harness despite the previous claim of inferiority.
segments.s320-g47: سپاسم ز یزدان is the fixed phrase “my thanks to God” (سپاس = thanks, ز = from/through). که introduces a clause of reason/explanation: او داد فر = “he gave farr (royal glory).” داد is the past tense “gave” and فر is the technical Pahlavi/Persian term for divine royal glory. The clause explains the reason for the speaker’s thanks.
segments.s320-g48: همان is an emphatic demonstrative “that very” / “the same.” گردش is a noun “turning/rotation/motion” governing a short triad: اختر و پای و پر — “star and foot and wing” (three coordinated nouns). The sequence is poetic: گردشِ اختر و پای و پر = “the movement/turning of the stars, of the foot, and of the wing,” i.e. a sweeping image of motion that the given فر orders or governs.
segments.s320-g1: This is an adverbial-temporal phrase: بر این‌گونه (an initial manner phrase, 'in this way/thus') + تا سالیان شصت (lit. 'up to sixty years'). The verb گشت (past of گشتن) appears after the temporal phrase and here has the poetic sense 'turned/passed' — i.e. 'it passed/it turned out so for sixty years.' Word order is typical older/poetic Persian (adverbial phrase + duration + past verb).
segments.s320-g2: This clause states the main result: جهان شد (literally 'the world became') followed by the predicate همه شاه را زیردست. Here شاه را uses the object/definite marker را to mark the person possessing control, and زیردست is a predicative adjective meaning 'under (his) hand/control.' The literal structure is 'the world became (all) under the king' → 'the whole world came under the king's control.'
segments.s320-g3: پراندیشه شد is a predicative phrase: پراندیشه (an adjectival/poetic compound meaning 'full of thought/overwrought, anxious') + شد (past 'became'). It is followed by مایه‌ور جان شاه, where مایه‌ور functions as 'rich/filled with' and جان شاه ('the king's life/soul') is the noun being described. The whole unit means 'the king's soul became filled with anxiety/care.' Note the older word order places the predicative adjective before شد and the possessed noun after the predicate.
segments.s320-g4: از introduces cause/reason here ('from/because of'). آن (that) modifies both رفتن کار ('the going/departure of the work/office' — a verbal noun construction where رفتن = 'the going/withdrawal') and دستگاه ('that court/establishment'). کار is 'affair/office' and دستگاه is the royal court/establishment; together the phrase means 'because of that loss/departure of (his) office and that court.'
segments.s320-g5: همی is an iterative/progressive particle meaning 'kept / continually' (he kept saying). گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical simple past). ویران و آباد are two contrasting adjectives ('ruined' and 'prosperous') that together modify بوم ('land/homeland'). The sequence shows the speaker repeatedly pronouncing the paradoxical description 'ruined and prosperous' of the land.
segments.s320-g6: A simple enumerative prepositional series introduced by ز (poetic 'from'): ز چین و ز هند و ز توران و روم lists geographic origins/regions ('from China and from India and from Turan and Rome'). Repeated ز is a poetic device; و is the coordinating 'and.'
segments.s320-g7: هم از خاوران تا در باختر is a span: هم 'also/even' + از خاوران ('from the East') + تا ('as far as') + در باختر. Here در means 'gate' (در = door/gate) and باختر 'the west' — so 'from the East as far as the western gate' (i.e. east to west). تا marks the extent of the range.
segments.s320-g8: Another enumerative prepositional phrase introduced by ز: ز کوه و بیابان ('from mountain and wilderness/desert'). The following token وز is a fused poetic form (effectively و + ز, 'and from') introducing خشک و‌تر — here خشک = 'dry' and و (written as و‌تر in the line) coordinates the following term; the pair خشک و تر contrasts 'dry and moist/wet' (i.e. every sort of land). The line therefore lists places 'from mountain and plain, from dry and wet' meaning all regions.
segments.s320-g9: سراسر is an adverb meaning “throughout / all over” placed before the prepositional phrase. ز + بدخواه with the verb تهی کردن (کردم ... تهی) expresses ‘to make empty of / to rid (of)’. کردَم is the first‑person past (“I made / I caused”) and تهی is the complement meaning “empty / devoid (of)”; together (ز بدخواه کردم تهی) = “I cleared (it) of ill‑wishers.”
segments.s320-g10: مرا (here split orthographically) is the indirect/object pronoun ‘to me / for me’; گشت is past/inchoative ‘became / came to (someone)’. The two nouns فرمان و گاه مهی are the things that ‘became’/‘came’ to the speaker: فرمان = “command” and گاه مهی = “the moonly/ lunar time” (مهی modifying گاه). The order ‘مرا گشت ...’ is an archaic/poetic way to say ‘it became to me’ = ‘I obtained’.
segments.s320-g11: جِهان is the subject ‘the world’. از + بداندیش is a prepositional/causal phrase (‘from / because of the ill‑minded’) and بی‑بیم شد is the predicate: بی‑بیم (an adjectival compound) = ‘fearless’, شد = past/inchoative ‘became’. Word order places the causal phrase before the adjectival predicate in typical poetic compression.
segments.s320-g12: دل اهرمن is the subject ‘Ahriman’s heart’ (دل + proper name). ز این = ‘by/through this’ (prepositional cause). به دو نیم شد is a periphrastic inchoative/passive: به … نیم = ‘into two halves’ (به used as ‘into/towards’), شد = ‘became / was made’, so the clause means ‘the heart of Ahriman was split into two by this.’
segments.s320-g13: ز یزدان = ‘from the gods’ (prepositional source). همه آرزو is the direct object ‘all / every desire(s)’ placed before the verb in poetic word order. یافتم is the first‑person past ‘I obtained / I found.’
segments.s320-g14: وگر introduces a concessive/contrasting clause (‘and yet / but’). دلِ همه = ‘my whole heart’ (دل + emphatic همه). سوی کین = ‘toward vengeance’ (سوی = toward, کین = vengeance/hatred). تافتم is a past form of the root تافتن used here as ‘turned / was turned’ (i.e. the heart turned toward vengeance). Word order is topical (دل همه … سوی کین تافتم).
segments.s320-g15: روانم = ‘my soul’ (possessive suffix). نباید is impersonal negative ‘must not / should not’. که introduces the subordinate clause (‘that / which’). آرد is third‑person present/subjunctive ‘brings / leads’. من (here part of old/clitic form spelled من + ی) is the object ‘me’; the final orthographic ی is an archaic/enclitic element. Overall: ‘My soul should not (be such) that it brings me …’.
segments.s320-g16: بداندیشی = ‘malice / ill‑thinking’ (noun). و links the two objects. کیش آهرمنی = ‘the Ahrimanic creed/sect’ (کیش = creed/sect, آهرمنی adjectival ‘Ahrimanic / demonic’ placed after the noun in the usual Persian order). This coordinated phrase is the object of آرد above (‘bring me into malice and the demonic creed’).
segments.s320-g81: بگردان is an imperative (ب + گردان) meaning “turn away / remove.” ز introduces the source (“from”), and جانم is جان + enclitic ـم (“my soul”). بد روزگار is a noun phrase “evil/bad fate” where بد qualifies روزگار; the clause therefore means “remove ill-fate from my soul.”
segments.s320-g82: همان چاره is a short demonstrative noun phrase “that same remedy/expedient.” دیو آموزگار places two nouns together: دیو modifies or is apposed to آموزگار, yielding “the devil (as) teacher” or “the demon’s teaching” — here meaning the remedy/expedient is of the devil. The verb ‘is’ is omitted (copula drop) as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s320-g83: بدان is made of the prefix ب + دان (imperative/command of دانستن ‘to know’) and functions as “know/consider this.” تا introduces the purpose/result clause (“so that”). چو here means “as / like,” followed by the three proper names (کاووس و ضحاک و جم) used as examples.
segments.s320-g84: نگیرد is the negative third‑person present/subjunctive form of گرفتـن (“may not seize / must not take hold”). هوا here means “desire/passion.” بر روانم is a prepositional phrase “upon my soul,” and ستم is the result noun “oppression/injustice.” Together: “so that desire may not seize my soul and oppress.”
segments.s320-g85: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause (“when/if”). بر من indicates the action affects “me.” بپوشد is the present/subjunctive/3sg form of پوشیدن used transitively here meaning “covers/veils/closes.” در is the noun “door,” and راستی is ‘truth/uprightness’ (here: the “door of righteousness”).
segments.s320-g86: به نیرو is an adverbial phrase “by force / with strength.” شود (شد) is the present/future third‑person singular “becomes / happens.” کژی و کاستی are the resulting nouns “crookedness (wrongness) and deficiency.” The clause states that wrong and lack come about by force.
segments.s320-g87: بگردان is an imperative “turn away / remove.” ز من marks source “from me.” دیو را دستگاه is a possessive/genitive-like phrase with the object marker را after دیو: literally “the device of the demon” (دستگاه = contrivance/device). The whole segment is an imperative plea to remove the demon’s contrivance from the speaker.
segments.s320-g88: بدان (ب + دان) again = “know this.” تا introduces purpose (“so that”). ندارد is the negative present of داشتن “does not have,” here functioning idiomatically with تباه to mean “may not be ruined.” روانم is “my soul” and تباه “ruined/corrupt.” Together: “know this so that my soul is not ruined.”

sentences.s320-s45: O guardian, keep me on this same path and manner, and lead my soul to that place of the righteous.
sentences.s320-s13: If I turn to crookedness and an evil path, the divine farr will be torn from me.
sentences.s320-s14: After that I shall pass beyond that darkness, and my head and helmet will fall to the ground.
sentences.s320-s15: A bad name of mine will remain in the world; before God the outcome will be the same—bad.
sentences.s320-s16: I shall be ruined; my face and complexion will slacken, and my bones will fall into the earth.
sentences.s320-s9: May I become like Zahhāk the Arab and Jam, who, with Salm and Tūr, come into the world.
sentences.s320-s10: On one side, if I have Kāvus as ancestor; on the other side, Turan is full of alchemy.
sentences.s320-s11: Like Kāvus and like the sorcery of Afrāsiāb, who in sleep saw nothing but a crooked visage.
sentences.s320-s12: May I, before God, for even a moment become ungrateful, and cast fear into the bright‑souled.
sentences.s320-s17: When virtue dwindles, ingratitude takes its place; the spirit grows dark and departs to another realm.
sentences.s320-s18: Someone has seized my crown and throne, and has brought my fortune down to their feet.
sentences.s320-s19: From me there remains a bad name as a memorial; the flower of ancient sufferings has become a thorn.
sentences.s320-s20: Now, when I sought to avenge my father, I would have set the world in order with goodness.
sentences.s320-s33: He shut that gate of the royal court; the open‑hearted one came in, roaring.
sentences.s320-s34: For the sake of worship he washed his head and body; by the candle of wisdom he sought the way to God.
sentences.s320-s35: Then he put on a new white garment; praying, he went with a heart full of hope.
sentences.s320-s36: He came stately to the place of prayer, and kept speaking the pure secret to the Judge.
sentences.s320-s29: We arrived and saw the secrets of the world — bad and good, both manifest and hidden.
sentences.s320-s30: Whether a peasant or a trader we saw, in the end all passage is to death.
sentences.s320-s31: The king commanded the commander of the watch that whoever comes to this court...
sentences.s320-s32: Return him with kind words; seek out all the people, and do not be harsh.
sentences.s320-s37: He kept saying, "O you loftier than the pure soul, raiser of fire from the dark earth."
sentences.s320-s38: Look upon me and grant me some measure of wisdom; also give me thought of good and evil.
sentences.s320-s39: So that I may be before you and offer supplication; by these virtues I will increase (my devotion).
sentences.s320-s40: Forgive my past sin; deliver my household from crookedness.
sentences.s320-s25: Now the time has come that I should become a seeker and stand before God with a face full of water (i.e. in tears).
sentences.s320-s26: Might there not also be, hidden within this goodness, a worshipper of the Creator of the world?
sentences.s320-s27: May my soul be carried to that place of the righteous, for this fair crown and throne will pass away.
sentences.s320-s28: No one attains from this overflowing abundance desire and renown, greatness and goodness, peace and the cup.
sentences.s320-s21: I killed anyone who ought to be killed — those evil, perverse, and rude toward God's way.
sentences.s320-s22: There remained not a single tree, in town or in ruin, that did not read the inscription of my throne.
sentences.s320-s23: The great ones of the world are inferior to me, and though they possess treasure and helmets, ...
sentences.s320-s24: I thank God, for he gave the royal glory (farr), that very turning of the stars, the foot, and the wing.
sentences.s320-s1: Thus sixty years passed, and the whole world came under the king's control.
sentences.s320-s2: The king's soul became full of care and sorrow because of that loss of office and of that court.
sentences.s320-s3: He kept saying that the homeland was both ruined and prosperous, from China and from India and from Turan and Rome.
sentences.s320-s4: Also, from the East to the western gate, from mountain and wilderness, and from the dry and the wet (everywhere).
sentences.s320-s5: I cleared everywhere of ill‑wishers; command and the moon's hour came to me.
sentences.s320-s6: The world became fearless of ill‑minded folk; Ahriman’s heart, because of this, was split in two.
sentences.s320-s7: From the gods I obtained every desire; yet my whole heart turned wholly toward vengeance.
sentences.s320-s8: My soul must not be such as to lead me into malice and the Ahrimanic creed.
sentences.s320-s41: Turn away from my soul the ill fate; that very expedient is the devil’s teaching.
sentences.s320-s42: Know this so that, as with Kavus, Zahhak, and Jam, desire may not seize my soul and bring oppression.
sentences.s320-s43: When it closes to me the door of righteousness, crookedness and deficiency arise by force.
sentences.s320-s44: Turn away from me the demon’s device; know this so that my soul will not be ruined.
